title: Population statistics – user guide | Stats NZ
content:
## Population statistics – user guide
26 January 2021, 10:45am
This page provides an overview of the different population statistics produced by Stats NZ, including census counts, population estimates, and population projections.
Collectively, these statistics give information about past, current, and potential future population changes.
This information helps governments, communities, and businesses evaluate trends and patterns, how past decisions and policies have performed, and how future decisions and policies should be shaped.
Find the latest population statistics and releases .
- Key population statistics
- Census counts Overview Geographic levels available Frequency Census response and coverage
- Geographic levels available
- Census response and coverage
- Population estimates Overview Geographic levels available Frequency Basic components underlying population estimates Discontinuity in 1991 population estimates
- Geographic levels available
- Basic components underlying population estimates
- Discontinuity in 1991 population estimates
- Population projections Overview Geographic levels available Frequency Assumptions underlying population projections Alternative projections Accuracy
- Geographic levels available
- Assumptions underlying population projections
- Alternative projections
- How to use population projections Projections are not predictions The future is uncertain So how might someone use the projections?
- Projections are not predictions
- The future is uncertain
- So how might someone use the projections?
- Explanations of selected terms
- Relationship between population statistics and other measures
- Customised projections

## Key population statistics
Key population statistics produced by Stats NZ include census counts, population estimates, and population projections.
The Census of Population and Dwellings is a five-yearly survey providing a wealth of data for:
- small geographic areas
- numerous variables including occupation and country of birth
- individuals, families, households, and dwellings.
In between censuses, population estimates by age-sex are prepared to give an indication of change since the last census. The estimates also include people not counted by the census, either because they were temporarily overseas or missed by the census.
For those interested in planning, projections (of population, households, families, and labour force) are derived to give an indication of future change.
The differences between these measures with reference to a subnational area can be illustrated in the following diagram.
Conceptual inclusions in key population statistics
Text alternative for diagram Conceptual inclusions in key population statistics
Additional information on these key population statistics can be found in the sections below:
- Census counts (‘census night population count’ and ‘census usually resident population count’)
- Population estimates (‘estimated resident population’)
- Population projections (‘projected resident population’).
Information about the Census of Population and Dwellings, the official count of people and dwellings in New Zealand.
- Geographic levels available
- Census response and coverage.
The census is the official count of how many people and dwellings there are in New Zealand. It takes a snapshot of the people in New Zealand and the places where we live.
Census usually resident population – DataInfo+ provides the most commonly used census measure. This is a count of all people who usually live in New Zealand, or in an area of New Zealand, and are present in New Zealand on a given census night.
This count excludes visitors from overseas and residents who are temporarily overseas on census night. For a subnational area , this count excludes visitors from elsewhere in New Zealand (people who do not usually live in that area), but includes residents of that area who are temporarily elsewhere in New Zealand on census night (people who usually live in that area but are absent).
The census night population count is a count of all people in New Zealand, or in an area of New Zealand, on a given census night. This count includes visitors from overseas and excludes residents who are temporarily overseas on census night.

### Geographic levels available
Census counts are available for geographic areas as small as meshblocks . Meshblocks are usually aggregated to larger geographic areas because of confidentiality provisions and to include additional census variables (for example, age, ethnicity, labour force status).
From the 2018 census, the SA1 statistical geography replaces the meshblock as the lowest standard published output.
Further information on the geographic levels for census data is included in the 2023 Census data user guide .
A census has traditionally been held in New Zealand every five years, typically in early March. Up until 2006 it was held in years ending in 1 or 6. The 2011 Census was cancelled due to the Canterbury earthquakes. A census was held in 2013, 2018, and 2023.

### Census response and coverage
The Post-enumeration Survey (PES), run shortly after the census, estimated that the 2023 Census counted 98.9 percent of the population of Aotearoa New Zealand, which corresponds to a 1.1 percent net census undercount . Previous iterations of the PES measured 97.4 percent coverage in the 2018 Census, 97.6 percent in 2013, and 98.0 percent in 2006.
These values are comparable with other countries. For example, Australia had 99.3 percent coverage in its 2021 Census, 99.0 percent in 2016, and 98.3 percent in 2011. Canada reported 96.9 percent coverage in 2021, and 97.7 percent in 2011.
In addition to net census undercount, each census also has some level of non-response. This was mitigated through the use of administrative data in the 2018 and 2023 Censuses, and substitute records in earlier censuses. More details can be found in Using a combined census model for the 2023 Census .
The PES provides official measures of both census coverage and response rates. These vary across different sub-populations; for example, males tend to have lower coverage rates than females. More detail can be found at 2023 Post-enumeration Survey .
Those people missed by the census are often in population sub-groups important to government policy formulation, so Stats NZ makes concerted efforts to maximise census coverage.
The net census undercount measured by PES is also used as a critical input to the estimated resident population (ERP).

## Population estimates
Information about population estimates, the best measure between census dates of the population that usually lives in New Zealand.
- Geographic levels available
- Basic components underlying population estimates
- Discontinuity in 1991 population estimates.
Population estimates, or the estimated resident population (ERP), provide the best measure of the number of people usually living in New Zealand between censuses. These estimates include adjustments to account for net census undercount and residents temporarily overseas , meaning that they are generally higher than the census usually resident population count .
Population estimates are updated regularly to account for births, deaths, and net migration (external and internal) since the last estimate.
The estimated resident population measure was introduced after the 1996 Census when a post-enumeration survey was conducted for the first time.

### Geographic levels available
Population estimates by age-sex are published at the Statistical Area 2 and 3 (suburb) level, as well as for larger geographic areas such as territorial authority and Auckland local board (TALB) and regional council areas. The most detailed subnational estimates are in Aotearoa Data Explorer , and the most detailed national estimates are in Infoshare .
National population estimates are produced quarterly (reference dates at 31 March, 30 June, 30 September, and 31 December) and provisional estimates are available about seven weeks after the reference date.
Subnational population estimates are produced annually (reference date at 30 June), and provisional estimates are available in mid-October.
Both national and subnational population estimates remain provisional for up to two years and are revised to incorporate revisions to international (external) migration estimates.
After each census the population estimates for the preceding intercensal period (between censuses) are revised. For example, following the release of results from the 2023 Census and the 2023 Post-enumeration Survey, a new estimated resident population at 30 June 2023 was calculated. This new base is used to revise the previously published population estimates for the period 2018–2023.

### Basic components underlying population estimates
The estimated resident population of an area is derived by:
- updating the census usually resident population count (from the most recent census) to take account of net census undercount and residents temporarily overseas on census night; and
- updating for births, deaths, net external migration , and net internal migration between the census date and the date of the estimate.
Birth and death data are obtained from birth and death registration forms, which have a high level of coverage and general accuracy.
Coinciding with the removal of the departure card in November 2018, official measures of international migration are now outcomes-based not intentions-based. This requires measuring the cumulative time spent in or out of New Zealand after people cross the border. For more information see the migration data transformation project .
Internal migration has historically been estimated using a range of symptomatic data including electoral enrolments, school rolls, residential building consents, and primary health organisation enrolments supplemented by historical internal migration and census data, and any relevant information supplied from each local authority. For the latest methods, see the latest subnational population estimates .
Further information is available on subnational population estimates .

### Discontinuity in 1991 population estimates
There is a discontinuity in national population estimates in 1991, caused by a shift from ‘de facto’ to ‘resident’ population estimates.

## Population projections
Information about population projections, the indication of future population change based on assumptions about future birth rates, death rates, and net migration.
- Stats NZ’s demographic projections
- Geographic levels available
- Assumptions underlying population projections
- Alternative projections
Population projections (‘projected resident population’) use population estimates as a starting point and are an indication of future population change based on assumptions about future demographic behaviour (birth rates, death rates, net migration).
Population projections are a tool to assist planning for the future. While information about the past and current size and structure of the population is useful, information about the likely future size and structure of the population helps government and communities to plan infrastructure and facilities to meet the needs of a changing population.
The projections are neither forecasts nor predictions, but an indication of likely future change given specific assumptions about future fertility (births), mortality (deaths), and migration based on current policy settings.
Further information is available in the statistical standard for population terms .
Stats NZ has produced population projections since the 1950s. As with other statistics produced by Stats NZ, there are advantages of having the independence and integrity of a national statistical organisation producing population projections.
There is also the advantage of having a consistent set of projections produced for every area of New Zealand – consistency in terms of methods and consistency with national-level projections – rather than a fragmented set of projections produced by or for different local authorities.
Stats NZ’s demographic projections suite
Text alternative for diagram Stats NZ’s demographic projections suite

### Geographic levels available
Population projections by age-sex are published at the Statistical Area 2 and 3 (suburb) level, as well as for larger geographic areas such as territorial authority and regional council areas.
The most detailed projections are in Aotearoa Data Explorer .
Stats NZ also produces customised population projections under geographies related to the health system, such as district health board (DHB ) areas . These projections are commissioned using tailored assumptions and classifications and are not therefore official projections published by Stats NZ (see Te Whatu Ora/Health NZ ) .
Demographic projections are updated and published every 2–3 years to ensure they reflect the latest demographic developments at the local and national level, and to maintain their relevance and usefulness.
The first projections released after the 2023 Census were National population projections: 2024(base)–2078 published in June 2025.

### Assumptions underlying population projections
Population projections are derived from an assessment of historical, current, and likely future trends in births, deaths, and migration – the three components of population change.
Assumptions about future fertility (births), mortality (deaths), and migration are formulated after analysis of short-term and long-term historical trends, government policy, and information provided by local planners.
Assumptions are set first at the national level and used as a constraint for the subnational assumptions. This ‘top-down’ approach helps prevent implausible projections for any area and enables higher level projections to be published quickly, without waiting for lower level projections to be completed first.
Fertility assumptions for each area are formulated in terms of age-specific fertility rates for each time period. The rates are based on the recent number of registered births in each area. The rates are then applied to the (female) population in each area to give the number of births for each time period.
Mortality assumptions for each area are formulated in terms of male and female age-specific survival rates for each time period. The rates are based on the recent number of registered deaths in each area. The rates are then applied to the population in each area to give the number of people who survive each time period (the number of deaths is calculated indirectly).
The assumed net migration level and age-sex pattern for each area is based on a consideration of observed past patterns, the capacity of the area for further growth (for areas with net inflow), whether historical outflows can be sustained (for areas with net outflow), and information available from and about local authorities relating to current and future developments which may affect population change.

### Alternative projections
Alternative projections help illustrate a range of possible population scenarios, but are not designed to cover every possibility.
Alternative projections are produced by combining different assumptions about the components of population change. A range of projection scenarios are produced (for example, low, medium, and high growth) to give an indication of likely future changes in the size and age-sex structure of the population.
Stats NZ considers the mid-range projection the most suitable for assessing future population changes. For stochastic national-level projections, the mid-range projection is the ‘median’ projection. For other (deterministic) projections, the mid-range projection is usually the ‘medium’ growth projection.
However, all published alternative projections are plausible. All the mid-range projections (that is, national, subnational; population, ethnic population, family and household, labour force) are produced to be consistent with each other.
Stats NZ does not make predictions but does produce a range of projection scenarios to give an indication of likely future changes in the size and age-sex structure of the population.
Demographic projections are not designed to be exact forecasts. Users can, and should, make their own judgement as to which of the projections produced by Stats NZ are most suitable for their purposes.
The projections are designed to assist both short-term and long-term planning needs and ‘accuracy’ is not necessarily the best measure of their usefulness. Indeed, the ‘accuracy’ of the projections can be irrelevant because users of projections, including central and local government, often use the projections to implement strategies to influence population dynamics.
This paper evaluates the accuracy of national and subnational population estimates and projections from 1996 to 2018: Evaluation of the accuracy of Stats NZ population estimates and projections, 1996–2018 .
The difference between projections and predictions is provided in an analogy made in Economic implications of an ageing Australia (2005) on the Australian Productivity Commission’s website. They give the example of someone seeing a large boulder on a train track. The projection is that there will be a rail disaster and many deaths if the boulder is not moved or the train is not stopped. The prediction is that someone will move the boulder, averting the accident. It is likely that the projection – the conditional forecast – is much more useful for policy formulation and planning.

## How to use population projections
Demographic projections are an information tool to assist planning for the future. They are one of the most certain types of ‘forecasts’ because of the gradual and comparatively predictable way that populations evolve:
- only births, deaths, and migration can change the population
- everyone ages one year, every year.
Eighty percent of the population alive in 20 years’ time, and all those aged 65 years and over (65+) in 65 years’ time, are already alive – only deaths and migration will change their number.

### Projections are not predictions
Projections are based on demographic trends and policy settings at the time the projections are prepared. They do not attempt to anticipate future policy changes. It would be counter-productive for the projections to second-guess what future policy changes may occur, given policymakers may be using the projections to inform those policy decisions.
Some policy changes will have a direct and obvious impact on population; for example, changes to immigration policy and approval numbers for residence visas. Other policy changes can have more indirect impacts, such as:
- changes to eligibility rules for national superannuation, which might affect labour force participation rates (labour force projections)
- regional infrastructure projects, which might affect city and district population growth (subnational population projections)
- local council zoning changes which might affect local population trends (SA2 population projections).

### The future is uncertain
The precise population level in future may be uncertain, but population proportions and distributions are often more certain. For example, the precise number of people in each age group and geographic area is uncertain, but the projections indicate with relative certainty:
- much higher shares of the population in older age groups (for example, 65+ and 85+)
- an ageing population in all areas of New Zealand and among all ethnic groups
- higher shares of the population identifying with Māori, Pacific, Asian, and Middle Eastern/Latin American/African ethnicities, and lower shares identifying with European/Other ethnicities
- a concentration of New Zealand’s population in the northern half of the North Island.

### So how might someone use the projections?
Recognising that the future is uncertain is important. As populations and policies change, the projections will also change. Use the following checklist as a guide:
1. Understand your purpose
What is it you are trying to measure? Is it purely a function of population numbers, or is population composition (for example, age, ethnicity) also important? Are there non-demographic factors you need to consider, such as changing costs, technology, or human behaviour that might impact demand?
2. Understand your population of interest
Most population statistics, including projections, relate to the resident population – the population that usually lives in an area. This is a good basis for many planning purposes. For example, this is the population that generally uses education and health services.
However, there are other populations to consider. For example, is the overseas or domestic visitor population relevant? There can be up to 400,000 visitors from overseas in New Zealand on any given day, ranging from tourists and business travellers here for a few days, to students and workers here for multiple months. Combined with New Zealand residents travelling domestically, visitors can place very seasonal and localised demands on services and infrastructure.
Is it population that matters, or is it the number of families, or households, or dwellings that matters most for your purpose? While change in these different concepts might be correlated, they won’t necessarily change at the same rate.
3. Choose the appropriate projections
The mid-range projection is internally consistent across the suite of Stats NZ projections and is a suitable indication of future changes at the time the projections were produced, but it is not a prediction. Depending on your purpose, using the mid-range projection may not be the best approach. Depending on the relative risks of under-projection versus over-projection for your purpose, consider using a higher or lower projection (or multiple projections).
Stats NZ always produces alternative projections because of future uncertainty, but also because a mid-range projection does not meet all needs.
- At the national level, these are stochastic projections, with uncertainty quantified. These give an estimate of the probability of population outcomes, but these probabilities are still based on current policy settings. The projections and uncertainties will be affected by major policy changes, pandemics, and catastrophes.
- At the subnational level, these are deterministic projections, with ‘low’ and ‘high’ growth projections as plausible alternatives to the mid-range ‘medium’ projection. These ‘low’ and ‘high’ projections are not formulated to encompass every possibility, so observed population change may well be outside the ‘range’ implied by these alternative projections.
4. Consider uncertainty
Uncertainty is not uniform and increases:
- the further into the future one goes
- for younger age groups, where the uncertainty of future fertility and migration impact more than at older ages
- for subnational areas, where the uncertainty of internal migration adds to uncertainty from external migration
- for ethnic populations, where there is the additional uncertainty of inter-ethnic mobility (people changing ethnic identification)
- for smaller geographic areas, where the populations are small and are sensitive to changes in demographic trends.
5. Be careful interpolating
Don’t assume that the population changes linearly between the start and end of the projections. Be aware of populations that might be growing and declining at different times over the projection period. Populations can also fluctuate in size because of birth cohort effects – different sized groups born in different periods moving through the age structure. Stats NZ’s cohort-component method takes this into account.
6. Use the latest projections
Demographic trends can change, with or without policy changes. Producers of projections like Stats NZ regularly update their projections, typically every 2–3 years, so check you are using the latest information.
7. Monitor how the projections are tracking
Stats NZ regularly publishes population estimates, which are based on observed or recorded data, and these are a useful calibration of the projections in the short  term. If you are in doubt about how to use different projections, you can email Stats NZ directly at demography@stats.govt.nz .
Evaluation of the accuracy of Stats NZ population estimates and projections, 1996–2018 has more information about retrospective assessments of projection accuracy. Past accuracy can indicate the future magnitude of inaccuracy, if not the direction of inaccuracy, for areas generally, but not specifically.
8. Update and revise your plans
Don’t rely on outdated information and have flexibility to incorporate new projections and other information when it becomes available.

## Explanations of selected terms
Definitions and explanations of selected terms used in population statistics produced by Stats NZ.
- Baby boomers, generation X, and generation Y
- Demographic transition
- Multiple ethnicity and ‘total response’
- People living in an area
- Sub-replacement fertility
- Visitors to an area and the ‘service population’.

### Baby boomers, generation X, and generation Y
Baby boomers are usually regarded as those born in the years 1946–1965, although the definition of the baby boom period varies between sources and countries. The baby boom was associated with high fertility rates and high numbers of births. New Zealand's annual total fertility rate was at least 3.5 births per woman during 1946–1965, and at least 3.0 births per woman during 1945–1972.
Generation X and generation Y are essentially marketing terms, common in the United States, and are less obviously tied to an observable demographic phenomenon, unlike the baby boomers. Stats NZ has no official or standard definition of generation X and generation Y.
If one subscribes to the 1946–1965 baby boom working definition, then generation X could be the 15-year period after the baby boom (1966–1980) and generation Y could be the 20-year period after generation X (1981–2000).

### Demographic transition
The demographic transition refers to the shift from relatively high fertility rates and high mortality rates to, first, relatively low mortality rates, and subsequently to relatively low fertility rates.

### Multiple ethnicity and ‘total response’
People can and do identify with multiple ethnicities. Official statistics use ‘total response’ where people are included in each ethnic group that they identify with. As a result, ethnic group populations overlap and cannot be summed to give the total population.
This approach is consistent with the findings from the 2004 Review of the Measurement of Ethnicity .
Prioritised ethnicity data are not recommended. Prioritisation re-classifies a person with multiple ethnicity responses to just one ethnicity or ethnic group.
There  are several problems with choosing one ethnicity for a person who has said that they belong to more than one ethnicity:
- Standard ethnicity questions ask a person to choose which ethnic group or groups they belong to. The question usually accepts more than one answer and prioritisation goes against the principle of self-choice.
- People are not asked to specify one main ethnic group and do not know that prioritisation would make this choice for them.
- Prioritisation misrepresents the number of people who identify with an ethnic group.
Other countries which ask a similar ethnic question allow multiple responses and do not use prioritisation (for example, the United States, Canada, Australia).
Stats NZ can provide prioritised ethnicity data as a customised request. For more information email info@stats.govt.nz or telephone 0508 525 525 toll free.

### People living in an area
In census statistics, a resident is a person who self-identifies on the census individual form that they usually live in an area. Guide notes for the 2023 Census included the following guidelines for usual address:
- If you live in New Zealand or are staying here for 12 months or more, excluding short overseas trips, follow these guidelines to give the right address: if you are a primary or secondary school student at boarding school, give your home address if you are a tertiary student, give the address where you live during term if you live in more than one dwelling, give the address of the one you most consider to be your home. If you spend equal amounts of time at different addresses, give only one of those addresses children in shared care should give the address where they spend most nights. If children spend equal amounts of time at different addresses, they should give only one of those addresses.
- if you are a primary or secondary school student at boarding school, give your home address
- if you are a tertiary student, give the address where you live during term
- if you live in more than one dwelling, give the address of the one you most consider to be your home. If you spend equal amounts of time at different addresses, give only one of those addresses
- children in shared care should give the address where they spend most nights. If children spend equal amounts of time at different addresses, they should give only one of those addresses.
- If you will be staying in New Zealand for fewer than 12 months, give your home country.
In international (external) migration statistics , up to the removal of the departure card in November 2018, a resident was a person who self-identified:
- on the departure card that they have lived in New Zealand for 12 months or more, and/or
- on the arrival card that they live in New Zealand and have been away from New Zealand for less than 12 months, and/or
- on the arrival card that they intend to stay in New Zealand for 12 months or more (or permanently).
From November 2018, the 12/16-month rule is used to objectively classify travellers crossing the border as migrants or non-migrants on the basis of whether they spend 12 months (or more) of the following 16 months in New Zealand. This rule has been used to generate a series back to 2001, and is now the official measure of migration:
- ‘Migrant arrivals’ are overseas residents, including New Zealand citizens living overseas, who cumulatively spend 12 of the next 16 months in New Zealand after arriving.
- ‘Migrant departures’ are New Zealand residents, including non-New Zealand citizens living in New Zealand, who cumulatively spend 12 of the next 16 months out of New Zealand after departing.
Migrant arrivals and departures include the flows of New Zealand citizens as well as the flows of non-New Zealand citizens as both affect the population living in New Zealand.
In birth registration statistics, the residence of the child is based on the self-identified ‘home address’ of the mother.
In death registration statistics, the residence of the deceased is based on their ‘usual home address’ as identified by the family and/or funeral director.
Population ageing refers to more people in the older ages both numerically and proportionately. Ageing is a gradual transformation of the age structure and is intrinsically linked with the demographic transition. Both the transition and ageing has occurred, or is occurring, in other countries, often at a much faster pace than is being experienced in New Zealand.

### Sub-replacement fertility
This refers to a total fertility rate below 2.1 children per woman ( replacement level ), which is the average number of children each woman is required to have for a population to replace itself in the long term, without migration.
Although fertility in New Zealand has generally been below replacement level since the 1970s, the population still has considerable built-in momentum for growth. The number of babies born today is partly predetermined by the number of females born 20–40 years earlier, while death numbers are largely predetermined by the number of births occurring 70–90 years earlier. Thus, despite sub-replacement fertility, natural increase (excess of births over deaths) has added an average of 30,000 people a year to New Zealand’s population since the 1980s.
Natural increase is projected to decline gradually but steadily over the next few decades. And as those generations who opted for low fertility enter the older ages, deaths will increasingly exceed births (natural decrease) in many areas of New Zealand (as is happening in many other countries).
An immediate return to high levels of fertility would not increase the number of women of childbearing age for several decades. Thus, just as it has taken decades for the full effects of sub-replacement fertility to be felt, it would take time for the population to overcome inertia and re-adjust to higher fertility.

### Visitors to an area and the ‘service population’
Population estimates and projections measure the number of people usually living in an area. They therefore exclude people who are visiting for holiday, study, or work purposes but usually live elsewhere.
Measures of the resident population are particularly pertinent for electoral purposes and some service provision (for example, schooling, libraries). For other services such as roads, sewerage, and health, measures of the service population – the population for which services are provided – may be more useful. The Ministry of Health’s Health Service User population is an example of a service population.
Defining and measuring the service population is problematic. The service population can vary depending on the service being provided. People may be counted multiple times in different areas depending on their mobility and use of services. And comprehensive information about different visitor groups (for example, commuters, seasonal workers, day-trippers) in local areas is limited.
For further discussion of these issues, refer to the Australian Bureau of Statistics working papers When ERPs aren’t Enough, 1996 and Service Population Pilot Study .

## Relationship between population statistics and other measures
Information about how population statistics can be influenced by other measures.
- Population and housing numbers
- Population and economic growth.
Indicators such as house sales, retail trade, electoral enrolments, school rolls, electricity use, water use, and car registrations can all change independent of population change.
Sometimes the indicators are influenced by visitors – people who usually live elsewhere and are therefore excluded from the population statistics for the area they are visiting.
The indicators can also be affected by changing economic propensities (for example, increased consumption per capita) or be driven by a segment of the population (for example, children, adults). This emphasises the importance of considering changes in age structure rather than focusing on total numbers of people.

### Population and housing numbers
Although houses are being built, this may not translate into more people living in an area due to a number of factors, including reduced occupancy rates (that is, fewer people per household on average), the building of holiday homes, and new buildings replacing demolished houses.
The changing age structure of the population is also important. In some areas the total number of people may be projected to decline. However, there may be opposite trends in the number of children (for example, decreasing) and adult population (increasing). Clearly, the impact of children and adults in the housing market is very different.
Stats NZ ’ s family and household projections indicate that households are growing faster than population. In some areas more households are likely despite fewer people. This is often driven by growth in the population aged 50+ years, who are more likely to live in one-person and two-person households than people at other ages. This reinforces the importance of the projections in providing information about the changing age structure, which is at least as important as changes in total numbers.

### Population and economic growth
The components of population change – births, deaths, and migration – do not necessarily relate closely to economic factors. This is because there are many more individual, local, national, and international factors which can interact and affect the population. Moreover, the number of people, families, and households may change independently of local economic factors.
It is important to note that demographic projections should not be confused with economic forecasts. Changes in the number of people, families, or households do not necessarily relate to the social and economic well-being of an area.

## Customised projections
Stats NZ provides a comprehensive range of demographic projections for all areas in New Zealand. More detailed information than  published on the website is often freely available, including more detail about the projection assumptions and projected age-sex composition.
Stats NZ can also customise the projections including:
- projections further into the future than published
- subnational projections for non-standard age groups (for example, 5–12 years, 17–19 years)
- subnational projections for intercensal years (that is, for years not ending in 3 or 8)
- projections for different geographic units
- projections incorporating different assumptions (for example, specific net migration levels)
- projections rounded to different levels
- projections in different file types (for example, xls, SAS, csv).
In some cases Stats NZ will require the client to agree to the specified assumptions and identify this agreement in any dissemination of those projections. For more information and quotes, email demography@stats.govt.nz or telephone 0508 525 525 toll free.
For feedback on this user guide, email demography@stats.govt.nz .
For general enquiries, email info@stats.govt.nz or telephone 0508 525 525 toll free.

## Text alternative for diagram Conceptual inclusions in key population statistics
The diagram presents the key population statistics produced by Stats NZ — these are the ‘census night population count’, ‘census usually resident population count’, ‘estimated resident population’, and ‘projected resident population’.
Census night population count includes visitors from overseas in the area on census night, residents of other areas of NZ in the area on census night, and residents of that area in the area on census night.
Census usually resident population count includes residents of that area in the area on census night and residents of that area elsewhere in NZ on census night.
Estimated resident population includes:
- residents of that area in the area on census night and residents of that area elsewhere in NZ on census night
- residents missed by census (net census undercount) which is estimated from a post-enumeration survey
- residents temporarily overseas on census night, and births, deaths, and net migration since census night, all of which are estimated from administrative data.
Projected resident population includes the estimated resident population at a given date and assumptions about future birth rates, death rates, and net migration which are estimated from administrative data.

## Text alternative for diagram Stats NZ’s demographic projections suite
The diagram summarises the different demographic projections produced by Stats NZ and their interconnections. National population projections are a benchmark for subnational and SA2 and SA3 (‘suburb’) population projections. These projections are also benchmarks for national and subnational ethnic population projections; national labour force projections; national and subnational family and household projections; and SA2 and SA3 (‘suburb’) household projections.
